turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 231, Salvatore Benanti, versus United States of America. Mr. Todaro.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: May -- Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. I don't want to transgress on the previous case but I had an idea with regard to these so-called beeps that are heard on the telephone. The reason for it is to indicate to the other party to the telephone that there is --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- a tap going on --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- and that's a very obvious reason therefore the person knows that there is a telephone message being tapped in an indirect manner.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: I wanted to get down to this case. In this case, the petitioner was convicted of violating the federal act in that he had in his possession, 11 cans of alcohol without proper stamps affixed thereto and in transporting those 11 cans of alcohol. A part of this situation, if Your Honor please, is that the police of the State of New York, pursuant to the authority of the State of New York and the law of the State of New York, had tapped a bar known as the Reno Bar. And on May the 10th, a message was intercepted wherein the petitioner informed someone else that 11 pieces was going that night. The officers -- the state officers thought that the 11 pieces meant 11 pieces of narcotics, and following that interception and that – that and having obtained that information, they followed the car and when they stopped the car and arrested the driver, instead of 11 pieces of narcotics, they found 11 cans of unpacked alcohol, thereupon -- thereupon turned over to the federal authorities for prosecution. Now, the heart of this question -- this case is whether a federal statute that prohibits both interception and divulgence of a telephone message and which goes on to the -- to prohibit the -- the use of intercepted message thus it's used in the federal court. It seems to me that every case that I've read up to the present time even this last case, the Rathbun case. They started with the principle that the state officer could not testify. Otherwise then they -- they wouldn't have to go on to the next question as to whether it constituted an interception.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The state officer could not testify in the federal court.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: In the federal court, yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is that so?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's exactly the point of this you're here, whether a state officer can testify in the federal court in violation of 605.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Now, in the court below, the court stated that -- that despite the fact that the state officer was acting pursuant to the laws of the State of New York and pursuant to a warrant of the State of New York, as soon as he testified under the federal court, he was violating the federal law, 605. Whether --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Do you say that's been held?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Do you say that's been held?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: By the lower court in this case, the Circuit Court -- the Second Circuit.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I thought the Second Circuit said that it's -- it's alright.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That it was alright to testify.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Yes. Oh, I thought --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: It was alright --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: It was alright --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- to testify but he said that as soon as the officer testified, he violated the federal --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- statute. Now, we get down to the question as to whether a state officer may testify in a federal court. We know, Your Honors, all know that the Nardone case, the first Nardone case and the second Nardone case have answered this question very clearly. In the first Nardone case, the Court -- this Court stated, the plain words of 605 forbid anyone unless authorized by the sender to intercept the telephone message and direct in equally clear language that no person shall divulge the message to any person, to recite the contents of the message in testimony before the court, is to divulge the message. The Act forbids that, such testimony. It thus does becomes a -- a play with words if we say that the Nardone represents the laws of the State of New York -- the United States as enunciated by this Court that by reason of the fact that this particular tap was made by state officer and the testimony is being made by a state officer that it puts it out of the protection of 605.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And in your point here that the -- that Act should make no distinction between state officers and federal officers or anybody else.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: The statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- is so crystal clear, Your Honor that it can't possibly make -- make any distinction. The statute says no person, not being authorized by the sender shall intercept and divulge a communication to any person. Now, no person, to my mind, is zero and you can't make anything out of zero but zero.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, assuming -- assuming that a state officer could not divulge the communication itself in a federal court, does it necessarily follow that he also can't divulge the fruits of the communication.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct. He cannot. That's the second Nardone case.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, that's what you got here isn't it? You've got fruits involved here and not the conversation itself.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Oh, no, Your Honor. If I may --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- if I may -- the Government in its –-

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I thought you had the fruits and not the conversation.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Oh, we have not only the fruit we have the whole tree and the conversation.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: You got it?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: This is the facts as stated by the Government. There is no dispute as to the fact, the facts are fully recited in the opinion of the lower court, that is, the Second Circuit Court. The Government in its brief here, in the recitation of the fact states as follows. “The New York City Police, believing the petitioner and his brother were violating state law by dealing in narcotics, obtained a warrant to tap the telephone of the Reno Bar in New York City, frequently used by the Benantis. On May the 10th", I'm reading from page 6 of the Government's brief, if Your Honor please. “On May 10th, 1956 the state police intercepted a message between petitioner and another person to the effect that 11 pieces was to be transported that night. So there was an interception of a message which gave the police a clue that something was going to happen that night. Acting on this information, the police stopped the car driven by the petitioner's brother, Angelo Benanti. They did not find any narcotics but did find eleven five-gallon cans of unpacked alcohol. Federal authorities were notified and this prosecution followed and it may be of interest to this Court that the lower court, the Circuit Court made this -- made this observation in -- in its decision. But it is equally clear that what, for the wiretap there would have been no basis for any prosecution whatsoever as the apprehension of Angelo and the seizure of the 11 pieces led to the discovery of appellant's participation in the violation of the federal law for which he had been convicted and the sequence, of course, in effect is clear. There is -- this is an (Inaudible) there has been an interception and the fruit of the poisonous tree or the tree itself was brought into court in the form of 11 cans of alcohol.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, the tree and the fruit, there was no testimony here, as I understand it, by the state officers as to what they heard over the wires, am I right?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No, no, Your Honor. If I may say – we -- we went up on -- on a very short statement of facts here because the U.S. Attorney and I agreed on this -- on the fact. There was a testimony by the district attorney of New York County who obtained the order from the New York Supreme Court to authorize the tap of this Reno Bar. The district attorney testified for the conversations. In fact, the Court wanted to caution me not to have it brought in because it involved the word narcotics. You understand? And the district attorney testified that there was a tap, that the conversation was that 11 pieces were going and that the car was followed and they said they found 11 cans of alcohol. The record is not completely, but it's conceded, it was was admitted in the –- in the lower court. It was so agreed that the U.S. Attorney's brief followed the same principle in the lower court and the lower court so found in its decision --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Who -- who put the district attorney on the stand, the Government?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: The Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The Government did?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And the Government questions elicited to what you've now told us?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Well the Government brought -- it meant to justify the -- the tap because they wanted to show that this tap was authorized --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: For whatever the reason is --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- it was on the Government's case --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: The Government's part --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- as the result came in?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's -- that's the Government's part. So though factually, there's no disagreement. There was no disagreement in the Circuit Court of Appeals and there's no disagreement (Inaudible) in the case. And the brief by the Government indicates that there is no dispute as to the facts. Now, in answer to the Justice's remark with regards to the fruit of the poisonous tree, the second Nardone case covers that. Before I get to that, in the first Nardone case, we have the clue and the answer as to why Congress passed 605. And this Court stated, the answer to the question is one of policy. The Congress may have thought it less important that some offender should go on with the justice than that the officers should resort to the methods, inconsistent with ethical standing -- standards and destructive of personal limit. Now, that I think is the crux of the thinking beyond Congress and by this Court that it would be destructive of ethical standards. Those are ethical standards that Congress adopted for the whole of the United States, not just a portion. Now, in the second Nardone case, this Court stated, the essence of a provision forbidding the acquisition of evidence in a certain way is that -- that merely the evidence shall not be used before the Court but that -- that it shall not be used at all. That it is no evidence, it cannot be admitted before the Court nor can it be used at all. That is strong language by this Court and it's sound language because it intended to do away with this -- the evils of wiretapping and that it intended --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: As to federal --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- to enforce the outcome.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: There were some federal officers in Nardone, too, were they not? Not state officers.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes, that's correct. That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Todaro, if state officers were guilty of illegal searches and seizures, stay away from this case for a minute.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Do they violate federal law?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes, sir definitely.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And yet Weeks says that they may testify in a federal prosecution.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: And the answer to that, Your Honor is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I was trying to ask you now try to distinguish --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: In the Weeks case, the violation -- the violation involved there was a Fourth Amendment, unlawful search and seizure. And the unlawful search and seizure being the Fourth Amendment, the first eight Amendments were prohibitions. They were limitations rather. They were imposed on the Federal Government as the price for ratification of the Constitution. Those were limitation imposed on -- upon the Federal --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But, apart from --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- that, were the state officers who were guilty of those unlawful searches and seizures, do they violate federal law?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No, because there is nothing in the Fourth Amendment that says that -- that the evidence C shall not be used in Court, that's only by judicial implication. The Court made that rule. In the Weeks case by judicial implication, the Court said if a federal officer makes an unlawful search and seizure it shall not be used in the federal court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, the jury's distinction here then, that the state officers in that instance are not guilty of violations of federal law were as innocent.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: They are.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The interception of the telephone call was a violation of federal law even though authorized under state procedure.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That is correct sir and the lower court so held too. Judge -- Judge Medina said the sooner the officer testified despite the fact that he did it pursuant to the laws of the State of New York, as soon as he divulged, he violated the federal law. And there's no -- no possible doubt as to that because the statute is so clear.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: So, what's the (Inaudible) then that the evidence is nevertheless admissible but the state -- that the state officer should be prosecuted for violation?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Oh, no. The evidence is inadmissible in the federal court. In a federal court, as soon as the officer testifies and divulges, he is committing a violation of 605 in the presence of the Court. In other words --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But the testimony was taken here.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes. And -- and I say Justice that as soon as the officer testified in -- in the Court and divulged the conversation, he was violating Section 605 of the Communication Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And you say that's what the Court of Appeals also had.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Why did the Court of Appeals allow the testimony in then?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Because they distinguished it -- I -- I think that the -- the mistake they made is they -- that they likened it to unlawful search and seizure. Now, unlawful search and seizure is not the same as 605. 605, is the statute's specific, crystal clear and admits of no doubts while the Fourth Amendment merely said that no -- no the -- the home of the person shall be secured against unlawful search and seizure. It doesn't say that the evidence ceases as a result of an unlawful search and seizure.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I -- I'll come back to it again.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: It cannot be used.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is the unlawful search and seizure by a state officer a violation of federal law or not, in your view.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: It is not.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: This intercept, you say, is --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- a violation of federal law.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And that's the distinction you make between this case and Weeks, is that it?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, why do you make that distinction? The Weeks case wasn't this case.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The Weeks case was the unlawful arrest and the unlawful search by a United States Marshal.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct. I'm referring to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the whole point of this case is --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct. I'm referring to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- was a there violation there?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: I'm referring to Wolf versus Colorado and Urban versus --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You said --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- California.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- you told me a different problem.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Weeks wasn't a state officer.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That was a federal officer.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And -- and the -- the evidence which he procured illegally, if you please, sought to be introduced because the cases are all the other way --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- as to the Weeks problem.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Whether the search and seizure is --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: But the Weeks case was federal law.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- illegal -- may – but rendered illegal by state law and not by federal law.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Am I wrong? I thought Weeks involved both federal officers and state officers.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: No. It involves --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Am I wrong in that?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- the federal officers, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You see, I've just read what the crucial thing is. The United States Marshal went in and searched the premises without a warrant.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct. Now --Because the later cases, evidently there are later cases mostly where the search is made without authority by a state officer and the state officers hand the stuff over to federal officers, there the Weeks doctrine doesn't apply.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's the Lustig case.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's what (Inaudible)

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: As in the Stemmer case but this doesn't apply to -- to 605.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not saying it does. I'm just trying to get --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- Weeks off of that.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: That's right and Irvine versus California. Now, there are some question with regard to the case of Schwartz versus Texas and that is being urged as an example as to why this evidence was proper -- properly admitted. But there is a distinction in Schwartz versus Texas. In that case, the State of Texas prosecuted the petitioner Schwartz for a state crime of robbery. The officer testified to a telephone conversation which was intercepted. One of the confederates in that particular case made an arrangement to have a telephone call with Schwartz and the officer would listen in and made a recording of that conversation. At the trial, the petitioner Schwartz objected to the introduction of evidence in the state court of that testimony because it violated 605. The Court allowed in an evidence and the case ultimately came up to the United States Supreme Court where they affirmed the conviction. Of course, I believe Mr. Justice Douglas dissented (Inaudible) In that case, this Court held -- held, we hold that 605 applies only to exclusion in federal court proceedings of evidence obtained and sought to be divulged in violation thereof. It does not exclude such evidence in a state court proceeding since we do not believe that Congress intended to impose a rule of evidence under state courts, we do not decide whether they have the power to do so.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: What case are you reading from?

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Schwartz versus Texas, 344 U.S. 199. The question can only arise in a federal court proceeding. Therefore, the Schwartz case clearly indicated that had this officer attempted to testify in the federal court, he would not have been permitted to do so. This question can only arise in a federal court proceeding. I have five minutes and maybe I ought to retain the five minutes for rebuttal.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I wish you'd help me out on one thing.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I wish you'd point to me where I can find what you say that the communication itself was testified, too, in here as distinguished from the truth.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Well, Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I can't find it.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: I -- you -- you will not find that particularly in the record. This was a short record --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: But we stipulated on the facts.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: We've got to decide this case (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: And if Your Honor wishes, I'll get a further -- I'll get -- I'll obtain the other --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, maybe there's no dispute.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: There is no dispute.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) the Government on it.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: There's no dispute because the Government --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: They just (Voice Overlap) they knew I -- I had it -- understood the case differently that the only thing that we were concerned with here was the fruits not the --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: If the --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- conversation itself.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: If Your Honor will read page 6 of the Government's brief, the first paragraph, page 6. I think that'd answer your question perfectly.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Page 6. Thank you.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: The first paragraph of -- of page 6. The New York City Police.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, I'm sorry it doesn't answer. To me all it says is what happened, it doesn't say that they testified to this at the trial. The reason I press you on this is because the -- your argument turns on the divulging aspect of this interception of communication.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: It turns on both grounds. It really would make no difference because even if there wasn't a divulgence, the very fact that there was attack made and evidence obtained in violation of 605, then it has the same effect under the second Nardone case it would apply. But here there was a conversation --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Second Nardone case, I suggest again involves federal officers and not state officers.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: But this involves a federal --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I'm not saying that you're wrong but I'm --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: This involves a federal court --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- with a federal prohibition, a very explicit federal prohibition, 605 which there's no question, shall intercept their communication or divulge its content. Now, on this -- as far as the facts are concerned, Your Honor, this is very clear. The -- the -- the Government says on May the 10th, 1956, the state police intercepted a message between the petitioner and another person to the effect that 11 pieces were to be transported that night, intercepted a communication, a message. That's very plain to me that they intercepted a conversation.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes but in there, I -- I'm not sure your court accepts this colloquy question.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Perhaps not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He doesn't deny that they intercepted. But he wants to know from you and I intervene because I'd like to know, too. At the trial, were the words which came over the wire those words themselves, they introduced.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: 11 pieces would go. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well maybe that page 6 of the Government's brief would indicate to the contrary.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Because it goes on to say the fact that --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: If Your Honor please --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the search came as a result of a telephone interception. It doesn't indicate that the interception of Mr. Davis will -- if he says he agrees with it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Well, may I point out Your Honor that in the opinion of the court below, the Circuit Court, the Court stated but if it's equally clear that, but for the wiretap there would have been no basis for any prosecution whatsoever.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That nearly proves that -- that doesn't disapprove that, what they got, that the consequence of the interception.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- gave them a lead and the lead gave them evidence in his conviction.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Now, if Your Honor wishes a further record on that, I'll be glad to supply it --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- but I think that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: -- it's conceded that -- that was so -- that was so.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You may reserve the rest of your time if you wish to.

turns.name: GEORGE J. TODARO
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, would you address yourself first to that -- that particular question? Did they testify at the trial to what the conversation was?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, if the Court please. On direct examination, the police officers were called to testify with respect to possession and transportation of the alcohol. They were called by the U.S. Attorney on the direct case. When the Government got through with the direct examination of the first police officer of the call, Mr. Todaro on cross-examination, may I say that there is no printed record in this case as it came up, you'll recollect the Court granted certiorari and it came up in this brief quickly so there is a transcript -- this is what I'm reading from here at the appendix in the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit, which is a copy of the transcript which is on file with the clerk, although it has not been printed because of no -- no opportunity to do so. Then, Mr. Todaro, on cross-examination said, "Officer, you were in the vicinity of this Reno Bar quite frequently?" "Yes, sir." "Did the Police Department have a tap on the Reno Bar, if you know?" And the police officers, "Yes, they have several taps on the Reno Bar." Then Mr. Todaro, on -- as a basis for objecting to the testimony and raising his objection brought out this information with respect to the tap and there is no question that when the next officer came up there was also the discussion with respect for the tap. This, however, was not a part of the Government's case. It didn't help to prove the Government's case anything that was in the -- in the conversation. It was brought out as a objection as the basis for an objection to receiving the evidence in that -- the information in evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Yes, but apart from that, was the subject matter of the conversation testified? Did it come out or as to it's ever intent -- did it come out of the trial or just a mere fact that there was a tap.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, there was a -- the language --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- about the 11 pieces was disclosed. Now, I think it was disclosed for the judge rather than for the jury in determining whether or not this evidence could be -- could be accepted. Let me see if I can find the 11 pieces. I don't think it's in this -- in this appendix. I think that was in the discussion with the -- with the Court as to the admissibility of the evidence as to -- by the police officers which was purported to be obtained from the lead of the -- of the interception.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, what I am -- I don't know why I have so much trouble about this. I must be stupid but as the case was submitted to the jury, was the jury -- did the jury have before it, testimony, as to what had been said in this tapped telephone --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- conversation. It did not.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, that's all I want to know. Therefore, our concern with here is the evidence as to the fruits of that conversation --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- and that is all, is that right?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why would that be true Mr. Davis if the evidence was or the statement was made on the evidence on the basis of the intercepted message and then prove what was in it and why was that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If that had been -- if that had been the case it would have -- then you would have a disclosure but the Government's case wasn't disclosure of the messages.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understood you to say that the judge, in passing on the admissibility of evidence as presented to us, the telephone with so-called intercepted the messages. Is it not?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, that's right. This -- this language the -- the 11 pieces, they -- they said this -- this information comes from a tap and the judge said let's see what the tap was and he went --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So, why -- why would the judge not be barred just as much as though it had been presented to the jury? Why -- why is it if any -- if it's wrong to present it as evidence to the jury to pass on the guilt from innocent --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- why wouldn't all --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If it -- if it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- be wrong, to present it to the judge (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If it is wrong --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- if it is not wrong, Your Honor, Mr. Benanti can't object. Mr. Benanti was pressing for the judge -- was pressing for the judge the fact that the Government's evidence should be excluded and the basis that he was saying it was -- should be excluded, was because there has been a telephone tap when he on cross-examination brought out there'd been a tap and then he brought this information to the judge as a basis for the exclusion of the Government's case. He cannot object now that -- that there was a -- an unlawful disclosure. He was attempting – attempting to -- too --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He's attempting to deprive the Government of the benefits of what he called an unlawful disclosure. And the only way he could judge was to show then he had that message on there.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- that is right. That's the only way that he could do it. And that's what he attempted to do. But now, the Government can't be taxed with the disclosure because the Government didn't want -- didn't have anything to do with -- actually, in this case, Your Honor the Government did not even know there had been an -- a -- a interception of these messages when it made the case. It was until cross-examination and the defendant brought it out as part of its defense, as part of its objection to the interference -- to the acceptance to this evidence that the United States Attorney knew that there'd been an interception.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So, your position then is different to what it would have been if the Government offered it as to the admissibility of evidence.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, if that would have certainly been a different case, yes. Then you would have had a question of whether there, there would have been a disclosure if you please. The Government would have been -- I don't say that would have answered the question but it has been an entirely different question because then if disclosure in the court is a violation of 605, the United States would have been involved in -- in that -- in that violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But Mr. Davis, I don't see how that helps you much because the second Nardone case says that the -- the fruit is just as excludable as the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- intercept.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- yes. Except that the second Nardone case does not say because the -- the 605 does not say that there is a disclosure. There is a divulgence when fruit, which is quite different from the message when the fruit is used in evidence. It would be --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't follow you --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- at all. I don't follow you at all.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) too said you can't use the product of a prohibited 605 transaction.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't that it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So the question that bothers Justice Harlan so much isn't decisive of this case or even very important to this case.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, we do -- well it's the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You must first establish that there was a violation of 605.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I think -- I think Mr. Todaro said that there would be a violation of 605 in this Court by the divulgence in this Court of this fruit. And I say whether or not it was admissible, it was not a violation -- nothing that the Government was presenting as evidence was a violation of 605. Maybe, if you apply the rule of the second Nardone case, this should have been excluded as the fruit of an illegal divulgence – an illegal wire-tapping.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But that doesn't mean that what is happening in court is itself a violation of 605.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, but I say that's immaterial to the essential question in this case.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It -- well, I'm not sure that -- well, I'm -- I'm not sure it's immaterial but it certainly is --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What I mean if you --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- if -- if --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We still have –-

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- we still have a case to decide.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If 605 was violated in this case then this evidence is inadmissible under Nardone and you don't prove that -- that 605 was violated by the inadmissibility of the -- of the fruit.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Davis --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- you do rely on Weeks, don't you?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I -- I wanted to mention Weeks because there's been some confusion -- almost all of Weeks deals with federal officials with the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes, but it also deals with state officials.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The last paragraph, the next to the last, except for the result, deals with a seizure by policemen by --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- local policeman and Weeks itself, is the origin of the rule that seizures by state police would -- state officials without the knowledge or concurrence of the federal officials may be introduced in evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, do you recognize the distinction between the Weeks problem that it concerned the state officers and the problem we have here?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, there is a difference because the Weeks case deals, of course, with the -- with the Fourth Amendment. And the question is whether the same rule shall be applied to the refusal to accept evidence because of a violation of 605.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And your position is there's no --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- difference in results they rely on Week's court.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We do rely on Weeks. We say that in the -- in the wire-tapping cases in general, the analogy has been drawn in -- in more than one case between the -- the Fourth Amendment situation, unlawful searches and seizures and wire-tapping. And that logically, the analogy should be followed through with respect to the interception by the state officer.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can't we get Weeks straight? Both of you rely on Weeks. I intervened in when your Brother was on his feet that Weeks that when we say Weeks, we mean the doctrine that the illegal obtaining of evidence by a federal official excludes that evidence for use in the federal court. That the Weeks also -- and we mean that -- that as against that -- the fact that state officials not collaborating or being the catch for or acting under federal authority obtain illegal evidence. It does not exclude the evidence. That's -- that's Weeks and the cases that follow.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, I couldn't possibly understand why your opponent relied on Weeks and why is the -- when you say Weeks you would mean the cases that follow, namely, Weeks -- the Weeks doctrine is a week of exclusion by misconduct on the part of federal officials.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right and it draws the distinction with respect to state (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And all the rest follows Weiss and all the rest.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And that is -- that is right. And, of course, the basis -- the basis of Weeks, as I think the basis here, could be that as justice -- as a court rule of -- in order to -- in order to -- to keep the -- the Court free from -- from encouraging illegal searches and seizures, we said we won't let you use them in our court because that would not -- that would encourage the -- the officers to -- to make illegal searches and seizures. If you -- if you slap their wrist on one hand and then use their evidence, why, you're not really preventing them from doing it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And that was said as to federal officers.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's been said as to federal officers.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But there the state officers, Weeks sanctioned the use of the material seized by state officers.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. And the reason is -- the reason is that you do not discourage the state officers from carrying on their state activities for state purposes, if you exclude it from -- from federal -- federal courts.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And do you concede that what the state officers did in the Weeks situation was not a violation of federal law in any respect?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, no. It was -- it was dealt with as -- as being illegal and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And the violation of federal --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And the violation of -- of -- as an illegal search and seizure. Now, of course, the Fourth Amendment doesn't specifically apply that to federal officials. So, it doesn't --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was it dealt with? I don't see why we have so much trouble. The Court said that – it has said that whatever the illegality now, they weren't acting under federal authority.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But -- what -- yes, that is right. And they said --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And -- but a state officer who -- who breaks in a house, is he violating the Fourth Amendment?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, he's not --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Certainly not.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BURTON
turns.text: So, when you get through with the Weeks case and it gets back to this case, what do you say to the facts of this case, will present or prohibit all divulgence and doesn't say anything about (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I will come to that in a minute, Mr. Justice Burton. But I'm assuming for a minute that it does apply to state officers. I'm assuming that 605, does apply to the state -- to --to the New York State Police but Section 605 does not deal -- Congress didn't deal with the introduction of the -- of the information in evidence. They said it shall be a crime. And the question is should it be received in evidence even assuming that it was illegally obtained?

turns.name: JUSTICE BURTON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And if we follow Weeks, then we say that since these are state officials that are involved in -- and that they were engaged only in local state enforcements that it can be used in a federal -- in a federal court even assuming it was illegally --

turns.name: JUSTICE BURTON
turns.text: And that's the view --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- taken.

turns.name: JUSTICE BURTON
turns.text: -- or the statute view that you don't have in the Weeks case.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. We have a statute but in the Weeks case, the question wasn't the legality or illegality of that seizure. It was a question of whether or not it was an appropriate thing to present it being introduced in evidence. Then they felt that it was not appropriate to exclude it from evidence when only a state official was involved.

turns.name: JUSTICE BURTON
turns.text: And they say the same thing here is what I'm telling you.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. I would say even assuming and I will come to a minute -- in a minute to the question whether it was illegal but I will say even assuming that the interception was in violation of 605 nevertheless, it can be received in evidence because on an analogy to the Weeks case, no federal official was -- was involved and no -- no public policy is --is furthered by excluding it.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Well, if the public policy involved of the federal court shall not lend themselves to aiding and abetting in a commission of a crime which is on your hypothesis involved under the statute.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, that it's not a -- those -- if -- if we did have a divulgence so that there was a crime being involved.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Yes, but your -- your premise as I understood it was in answer to Justice Burton that you were assuming that there was a violation as to --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: --the statute.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: If there is a violation of the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: A past violation, that is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Well, it's a violation of the statute in (a) the interception in your assumption and (b) in the divulgence.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- which has taken place in the past.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: No, which is taking place in the court room.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think that would be a different case, Your Honor if there were a divulgence --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: That's why I was pressing you so hard as to whether the communication was divulged.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's -- that's right. And I -- I understand that -- that point and I say it would be a different question if the divulgence did appear in the court room because there you would have the court sanction -- sanctioning a violation -- a current violation of -- of 605 --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Is that what you're saying, in effect, is that you can assume, (a) that there was a violation of the statute through -- by interception, (b) a divulgence, there would have been a violation if there had been divulgence of the intercepted communication --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- but it does not follow that where there is no divulgence as embraced in the statute to wit, not only the fruits you used, it doesn't follow that -- that there should be an inclusion.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. And then -- and then I think follows from Weeks and --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I'm not so sure about it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and Lustig --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: That's the point of it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and the other cases which follow which say that this rule of exclusion shouldn't be applied when the federal officials are not involved. And when --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There was a clearly --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- it's not so much whether they're state or federal officials really. It's a question of whether or not they were going -- they were involved in federal law enforcement at the time. One doesn't prevent people from continuing to do the -- their local law enforcement by excluding the information. All they're doing is -- is penalizing themselves in a particular case if they exclude it. It doesn't accomplish anything. It doesn't prevent the state local -- the local officials from continuing this activity.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But, Mr. Davis, was there any doubt there was a divulgence here?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There wasn't any divulgence the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not talking about the in-trial. Was there any doubt that what they got from the -- what they heard they divulged.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes. But there's no indication that they divulged it.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But they -- but they must have in order for the -- the truths could have been gathered from it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The -- the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- told

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in order to be tipped off.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The individual officers who -- who listened under the state -- under the state order went out and -- and stopped the trial and arrested the people. I'm not -- I don't know until Mr. Mr. Todaro asked the questions as to what happened in order to (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not talking about the trial.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But I'm not sure they divulged it at any time. They went -- they -- they heard that the -- that the car was going to be moving with 11 pieces and they went out and arrested the man. The very -- the man that listened.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And then they handed it over to federal officials.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They handed the alcohol -- the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- the -- the case over to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And they --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Not the -- not the interception which is --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, no. And -- but -- but you think that we can discuss this case on the assumption they just adhere we found it and then left the room.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We found this man carrying alcohol.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, that's the – that's the -- as far as we know, the federal officials had no idea that there was a wiretap. There's no --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- reason why they should have known it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, was there a pretrial motion for suppression of the evidence?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, no there was none.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: There was none. Only -- only an objection when -- when the evidence was offered.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well if (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And in that I'm not sure that there could have been because I don't know that -- that the -- the counsel who was called in late had any information as to what the case was going to be and tell them there's a trial until he heard it. I'm not -- I think that this might be a situation where the object -- objection had to be made at the trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, if it's -- if what you've said is so, there was no divulgence here and all you've got is handing over to the federal agency, stuff which the state officials got.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That's your case.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- that is -- that is this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And what --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and why all of these problems about Weeks never helped them to decide, if that's all there's to it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Because the claim of the petitioner here is that the lead -- the lead which led -- which resulted from the arrest came from wiretapping.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but if there was no divulgence, there was no violation of the 605.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- I think the exception is might well be.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What do you mean “might well be” if divulgence has to be a necessary part and the record is barren of divulgence, you cannot just say “there might be” --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- without any complying, opening the door to say “there was a divulgence.”

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If there was no divulgence, assume that.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could there possibly be a violation of 605?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, there couldn't. There has to be --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, there couldn't.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There has to be an interception --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- they couldn't (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- divulgence.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Was this Nardone, too, involved a divulgence of the conversation?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Nardone two, I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well the fruits, isn't it?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes but that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Except --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) interception was already in the first.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There was no discussion in Nardone two of -- of divulgence. There can be no question that many people knew of the contents of these intercepted telephone messages in Nardone. I mean --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but Nardone two is built on Nardone one, this is all the prior trial.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. I'd like to move, if I may, to the question of whether or not there was a violation of 605 because if there was no violation, then we've -- we have none of these problems. The New York statute is specific that -- it's -- it's found in its Constitution and its Code of Criminal Procedure and it specifically provides for the issuance of a warrant by a court on application under oath showing probable cause of the necessity of intercepting telephone message. The length of time which the warrant can be used is -- is limited by statute. It was under this particular application, under this particular law that this New York City police made this interception. They did apply to the New York Court. They got a warrant. They listened pursuant to the warrant. So, this case raises, very squarely, the question of whether or not that New York procedure is outlawed by Section 605 of the -- of the Communications Act. Now, it's also clear that Section 605, in language, covers everyone. If it's to be read literally, it applies to the judge who issues the warrants and the policeman who -- who listened to the call because it makes no exception for -- for anybody. But in dealing with a problem of balancing federal and state legislation, this intergovernmental problems, it is not infrequent to -- for the Court to interpret what appeared to be blanket prohibitions so as to permit the States to apply their local policies. Let me give you an example, an old example. There's been on the statute for many years a law which says that nobody shall interfere with the -- the carriage of the mails. Many years ago in Philadelphia, the local law officials found a man driving sleigh down Chestnut Street without any bells on the sleigh and proceeding in an unlawful rate of speed. And the local law official arrested the mail carrier. And the question came up before Justice Washington sitting on the Circuit, as to whether or not, the blanket prohibition against the interference with the mail covered the -- this interference by the local law official. And --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And this man, an employee or a contractor.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: He was an employee --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Employee?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I mean he was a regular --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Regular employee --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- as far as it appears, he was a regular employee. At least there's nothing in the case that would indicate that there was any -- anything different. This case that I refer to was the United States against Hart and I quote on page 16 of our brief from Justice Washington's construction of the Act to say that it would be unreasonable to apply the Act with this kind of a stoppage. Now, that's an old case. There's been more recent cases that -- that apply the same thing. One fairly recent case is the Penn Dairies case, Penn Dairies against Milk Control Commission in 318 United States where there was a blanket law that said that military installation should buy their -- their supplies by competitive bidding. It's a blanket regulation. The question of -- which arose here was whether the Penn Dairies which was subject to a local law as to milk control, setting prices, whether they -- whether the -- the competitive bidding requirement apply.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: What do you do with Weiss against United States that holds this Act as I understand it applies to intrastate communications -- telephone -- intrastate --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: -- wiretap?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, that -- I am not -- my argument -- all right so I got to accept it that I mean it -- it is the law and -- and it does not seem to me inconsistent with the position that I am taking. In -- it's been possible to -- to separate interstate and intrastate communications over an interstate system as the telephone system is. And when there is wiretapping, it maybe interstate and it maybe intrastate and 605 applies to both, at least this Section, this clause of 605 applies to both. But that does not mean that local state officials may not be exempted from the prohibition and I'm -- I'm not even suggesting that they are limited to the interception of intrastate communications because when they listened under one of these warrants, they can't tell whether it's going to be intrastate or interstate that they are intercepting. But what they are doing is, in -- in pursuant to the New York State's provisions for law enforcement and pursuant to a warrant of the Court, they are in New York and without preventing the -- the system from operating, they are listening in on interstate or intrastate messages to this -- to this bar. This, we say, since it is pursuant to a local law enforcement policy, is not forbidden by 605 of the -- of the Communications Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: That's the Schwartz against Texas or Schwartz against United States cut into that argument (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, Schwartz against United States says that -- no, it's Schwartz against Texas. Schwartz against Texas --

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: What it says --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- the State of Texas may, in -- in its courts -- in its courts -- in its (Inaudible) trials may use evidence which is obtained by federal officers.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Violation of the Act.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: In violation of 605 of the Act. Well, but not under any specific authority of -- of law, of Congress. May I say this, that the Nardone case itself, the first Nardone case says that there is no -- that it applies to law officials, as to others that the prohibition does, applies across the board to everybody. But it was not there dealing with a case of any specific government authority to intercept, as we have in this case. In Nardone, the -- the officers were -- were intercepting as part of their law enforcement but without any -- they were federal officers and obviously there was no specific authority given to them to -- to intercept. So, we feel that -- that the -- the fact that this Legislature of New York and specifically dealt with this problem, considered that it is necessary to their local law enforcement, should lead this Court to consider very carefully, whether or not these two laws can be reconciled or whether they are irreconcilable. If they are irreconcilable, of course, the Supremacy Clause of the Constitution must control and the -- the Constitution and the law of -- of New York must be held invalid. But one doesn't arrive at a conclusion like that if it is possible by construction to -- to let both of them stand together. In passing the Communications Act, Congress was very -- was very insistent that the State's interest in telephone communications should continue to be recognized that it felt this was a -- this was -- and utility in which they were both federal and state aspects and that the Federal Government – that the Congress was very careful not to preempt the entire field. They said we want to save for the States certain amount of regulation and certain amount of -- of local autonomy. And it seems entirely consistent with -- with that general policy, although they didn't deal with this problem -- specific problem before United States, specifically. It seems only consistent with that if they would have intended that a local law permitting interception under court warrant would be -- should be -- should be legal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Suppose Mr. Davis that instead of having a positive law such as New York had permitting officers to use these wiretaps say, they have the policy of permitting them to do it without having any law against wiretap, would the situation be the same here?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That would be much closer to the Nardone case itself. It would be possible in a -- in -- in that case to -- to draw a distinction when you have a state official doing it in accordance with the state policy. But by and large, the first Nardone case says that law officials, like anybody else, would be subject to the Act. And I think it'd be a very difficult case if you didn't have the specific authority.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So then they couldn't have if they couldn't have a policy of letting them do it by a lack of legislation how the -- how could they -- how could they cure the situation by legislation giving them the affirmative right to do it --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It can only --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- if it's in conflict with -- if it's in conflict with the federal law.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If it is in conflict with this federal law, they cannot do it. It's a question of interpreting --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- the federal law.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, I thought that -- I -- I understood you that it would -- you thought it might be --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- in conflict with federal law if they have no statute in New York and they relied on -- on the policy of letting -- letting the police officers do it by way of not prohibiting it. You thought that would be. Now if -- if that -- if that doesn't help them how will the statute help them?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it would only help them if we would construe 605 to mean that Congress couldn't have intended to prevent the States from dealing with a local problem of this kind through legislation. I am urging that it is possible as in this other cases by implication to -- just to -- to find a -- a authority to the States to legislate in this field.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is there anything in -- in the legislative history that would indicate that the Congress intended to prevent all federal officers from doing this and all citizens but intended to leave local law enforcement officers free to violate the law?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. There's no -- there's no indication of any such intent on the part of Congress.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Before you sit down Mr. Davis, I want to leave you a little uncertain of your hesitation -- this hesitation which was in saying there could be a violation of the 605 if the record is barren of a fruit of divulgence granting there was an interception.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I -- I don't hesitate because I have any question as to about what -- about our position that there has to be both an interception and a divulgence.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't mean your position really that but -- but 605.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, 605, says that. There must be --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And what is the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- interception and divulgence.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if the record is barren of -- of an interception --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Of divulgence.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Of divulgence. I beg your pardon. Where do we go from there?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I think there can be no violation but that I think that in -- the reason that I -- that I have difficulty with that case, this situation, is that -- that some of the language of the Court in dealing with this problem, deals with wiretapping, as such, as being an underhanded bad business. I think it comes from reading the -- the dissents -- the dissents in the -- on -- on the original Olmstead case. And so one has a feeling that if there is this kind of -- of interception in the case that we have a problem as to the -- the reception of the evidence and the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I should think if lower courts talk that way. I don't know anything about it there's all the more reasons for getting clarity into the business?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This is all the more reasons for your being more robust than you are in standing --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- on that position.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well I -- well I can assure Your Honor that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, I don't want to put you --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, I can assure Your Honor that there's no -- there is no hesitation on my part on that -- on that issue.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right, I --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But it's clearly our position that there must be both interception and divulgence before there's a violation of 605. There must be --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) 605 doesn't say or doesn't say anybody who intercepts (Inaudible) is -- be guilty and can be civilly, sued, does it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It does not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, we can't go on the general atmosphere that interception is bad business.God knows nobody thinks worst of it than I do.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. The statute requires both and -- and so far -- so far as I can ascertain, there's no -- nothing in this record with respect to divulgence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you Mr. Davis since the statute does require a rule, what do you say about what Judge Medina said on page 4A of what he thinks where he said we have no alternative other than to hold by tapping the wire, interception in the communications made by a telephone and divulging at the trial what they had overheard, the New York police officer violates the federal statute.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's what Judge Medina said.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So, are you challenging it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I find no evidence of any divulgence at the trial except as I had explained to the court as a basis for objecting to the introduction of the evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you challenge that statement in your brief? It is rather --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- it's rather unusual to the court to go against the -- what the court says as to the facts of case --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, we --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- we have not -- we have not Mr. Justice Black, we haven't argued in our brief in this case and quite frankly until Mr. Justice Frankfurter pressed me on this one. I hadn't realized the importance of this to the -- to the case. We haven't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- argued the absence of divulgence here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought (Inaudible) might have been the reason why you have the statement there.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- there.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- I -- one reason -- one reason that -- that this comes up and -- and maybe without proper preparation is that we don't have any proper record in this case because of the way this thing comes to this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That being true, wouldn't we have to accept

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- what the court held?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, you -- well we'd -- you have before you the transcript of the proceeding below which -- which will show you everything that was -- I mean there's no question as to what went on at the trial. That transcript is on file with the Court. So there's no --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He found that there was both interception and divulgence.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, he makes that remark. I don't know whether his mind was --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) at the Court's opinion, isn't it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's certainly in the Court's opinion whether it's a conscious weighing of any evidence or whether it's a -- an assumption, I don't know.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Davis, if at the trial either it's before the courts or before the jury, one of the officers testified to this conversation so far as 11 pieces are concerned. How can it be said that there is no divulgence?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think that that would have been a divulgence of that information, yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, it was. It was there and it was a divulgence, wasn't it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, but I -- but I don't think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well then --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- that the petitioner -- the petitioner can now object because on cross-examination in opposing the in -- the admissibility of the evidence can object because he brought out a message of this kind.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, you brought the fruits of it -- of it there and wasn't he entitled in his conscience to (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It's certainly wasn't entitled to. That was the only way that he could challenge it and that's the way he did challenge it. But that doesn't --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And indeed --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- mean that the Government --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- there was this divulgence then -- then --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And this --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- if there was a divulgence, then Judge Medina is right, isn't he?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, not a divulgence in the sense that should exclude the evidence from the -- from the -- should result in an -- sa violation.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, does the -- does the statute say that it shall not be divulged to a jury? It says it shall not be divulged.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, if there was a violation by the police officer in -- in answering this question in court, if there was such a violation, it is not attributable to the Government. It's attributable to the petitioner and the petitioner cannot then object to be introduced and introduction of some other evidence on the ground that he caused the divulgence.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, when you -- when you bring a -- a witness into the case and -- and produce evidence that amounts to the introduction of -- of poisoned evidence, can you hide behind -- behind that --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- and say that --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I'm not trying to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- we don't -- we -- we're not responsible for any divulgence because the -- the defendant tried to show and did show that it was illegally obtained?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We're not trying to hide behind it, Your Honor. We think we had an entire right to challenge this evidence and to bring out this -- and to bring this out before the Court. I mean he had to say --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- in order to challenge it he couldn't do anything else.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose a police officer had said or asked the court's ruling not to -- to be allowed not to answer this question because to answer it would commit a crime.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I -- I think [Laughs] it would -- it would be an impossible situation. I mean there'd be no way -- that would mean there would be no way of challenging -- challenging the admissibility of this evidence. If you couldn't talk -- maybe you didn't have to tell the details of the conversation. Maybe all they had to do was to say there was wiretapping. Maybe that would have been --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't see -- I don't -- myself --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And that wouldn't be a divulgence with -- of the message.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't see how the -- how it was necessary to tell that there's interception in order to tell that this was evidence.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, they wanted to find out whether this resulted from the interception. And in order to find out whether this seizure resulted from the interception, they have to find out what was said in the interception and that's why these questions were asked. And whether it was proper or whether it wasn't, I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What I'm suggesting --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- and I (Inaudible) the policeman might say I'm -- I'm familiar with the law and I'm -- my -- my lips are sealed because that would make a crime which heretofore wasn't a crime because there's been no divulgence.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And then we would come down to the question of who has the burden of proof showing that there is a connection between the evidence and the -- and the alleged interception whether it is the objector or whether it's the -- the Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I don't see how you get around what Justice Black pointed out. The record (Inaudible) Here we got the Court of Appeals saying it was divulged, what difference does it make and he was in -- it was divulged it was divulged. You're trying to use the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But -- but I'm not trying to use anything that was divulged, Your -- Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Well, the fruits. That's the difference --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But I'm not -- yes. What [Laughs] -- what I'm trying to -- what we're trying to use is the evidence which occurred before that and if there was divulgence, it was divulgence which the petitioner cannot obtain.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So, then you get down to a very narrow point. If you -- if you are -- if you have to live with this record, this statement then it seems to me what you said before indicate that you cannot use the conversation itself even though by a state officer in this proceeding. And then the only question is whether the fruits could be used --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'd say that's -- that's all there is. But you're -- you're bound by this (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Maybe it was our fault deciding the case, too.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I'm not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- sure. I -- I think we're bound by -- by the transcript which is before you --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But the thing is --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- of -- of what actually occurred. I mean --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And this is a finding by the Court of Appeals. How can we overlook that?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, if it --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Look at the record and see if it's going out.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If there's no -- if there's no word in --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- the record --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't see that we're borne by what an opinion (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If there's no word in the record (Voice Overlap) --
